hypothesis: Chemical foundation models pre-trained on large-scale SMILES representations can generate transferable molecular embeddings that, when combined with composition-weighted aggregation and device-level contextual variables, enable accurate prediction of battery material properties across multiple scales—from molecular electrochemical properties to device-level performance—outperforming traditional Morgan fingerprint baselines, particularly for out-of-distribution chemical spaces, because their pre-training encodes generalizable physicochemical knowledge that captures structure-property relationships relevant to battery applications.
